Medici family mystery may be solved after more than 400 years

By Jacopo Prisco
Updated Jul 16, 2026, 11:30 AM ET
The Chapel of the Princes, a lavish burial site for the Medici family in Florence.
See all topics
Link Copied!

Since the mysterious deaths of a husband and wife in the Medici family, rumors have swirled about what led to the couple’s untimely demise. Scientists now believe they have an answer: it was malaria.

A new study led by Serena Tucci, an assistant professor of anthropology at Yale University, and jointly conducted by Yale and the University of Pisa, Tuscany, used DNA extracted from the skeletal remains in an attempt to settle the debate once and for all.

Two species of malaria

Malaria is one of the great historical killers for humanity. The new study found not just one, but two species of the malaria parasite in Francesco’s remains, suggesting he could have had a double infection.

Scientists find first physical evidence that Romans used human poop as medicine

Historical sources supported the assumption that malaria killed the couple. Documents written by court physicians described symptoms consistent with the disease and treatments such as bloodletting.

Nevertheless, the research represents an important step forward and demonstrates how paleogenomics can contribute to addressing longstanding historical questions.
